instruction: Implement the Curiously Recurring Template Pattern (CRTP) in C++ to achieve static polymorphism, comparing it with virtual dispatch in terms of performance and design trade-offs.
output:
<think>
CRTP is a C++ idiom where a class derives from a template class parameterized on itself: `class Derived : public Base<Derived>`. This enables the base class to call derived class methods without virtual dispatch — the compiler resolves calls at compile time through static_cast.

Why CRTP over virtual functions?
1. No vtable pointer overhead (saves 8 bytes per object)
2. No indirect call through vtable (branch prediction friendly)
3. Enables inlining of derived methods
4. Better for hot paths in tight loops

Trade-offs:
- No runtime polymorphism (can't store different derived types in same container easily)
- Each CRTP instantiation creates a new base class type
- More complex template error messages
- Can't change behavior at runtime

Design:
- Base<Derived> provides interface methods that call derived().impl()
- The static_cast<Derived*>(this) is safe because the contract says Derived IS-A Base<Derived>
- We add a concept/static_assert to validate the derived class has required methods

Real-world usage: Eigen's expression templates, LLVM's CRTP utilities, game engine component systems.

I'll show both CRTP and virtual approaches for the same interface so the comparison is clear.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>
#include <cmath>

// ============================================
// CRTP: Static polymorphism (compile-time)
// ============================================

template <typename Derived>
class ShapeCRTP {
public:
    double area() const {
        return static_cast<const Derived*>(this)->area_impl();
    }

    double perimeter() const {
        return static_cast<const Derived*>(this)->perimeter_impl();
    }

    void describe() const {
        std::cout << "Area: " << area()
                  << ", Perimeter: " << perimeter() << "\n";
    }
};

class CircleCRTP : public ShapeCRTP<CircleCRTP> {
    double radius_;
public:
    explicit CircleCRTP(double r) : radius_(r) {}

    double area_impl() const {
        return M_PI * radius_ * radius_;
    }

    double perimeter_impl() const {
        return 2.0 * M_PI * radius_;
    }
};

class RectCRTP : public ShapeCRTP<RectCRTP> {
    double w_, h_;
public:
    RectCRTP(double w, double h) : w_(w), h_(h) {}

    double area_impl() const { return w_ * h_; }
    double perimeter_impl() const { return 2.0 * (w_ + h_); }
};

// Generic function works with any CRTP shape
template <typename Shape>
double total_area(const std::vector<Shape>& shapes) {
    double sum = 0;
    for (const auto& s : shapes) {
        sum += s.area();  // Inlined, no vtable
    }
    return sum;
}

// ============================================
// Virtual: Dynamic polymorphism (runtime)
// ============================================

class ShapeVirtual {
public:
    virtual ~ShapeVirtual() = default;
    virtual double area() const = 0;
    virtual double perimeter() const = 0;
};

class CircleVirtual : public ShapeVirtual {
    double radius_;
public:
    explicit CircleVirtual(double r) : radius_(r) {}
    double area() const override { return M_PI * radius_ * radius_; }
    double perimeter() const override { return 2.0 * M_PI * radius_; }
};

class RectVirtual : public ShapeVirtual {
    double w_, h_;
public:
    RectVirtual(double w, double h) : w_(w), h_(h) {}
    double area() const override { return w_ * h_; }
    double perimeter() const override { return 2.0 * (w_ + h_); }
};

int main() {
    // CRTP usage — homogeneous container, static dispatch
    std::vector<CircleCRTP> circles;
    for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; ++i) {
        circles.emplace_back(static_cast<double>(i % 100));
    }

    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    double crtp_total = total_area(circles);
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

    // Virtual usage — heterogeneous container, dynamic dispatch
    std::vector<std::unique_ptr<ShapeVirtual>> shapes;
    for (int i = 0; i < 1000000; ++i) {
        shapes.push_back(std::make_unique<CircleVirtual>(static_cast<double>(i % 100)));
    }

    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    double virt_total = 0;
    for (const auto& s : shapes) {
        virt_total += s->area();  // Indirect call through vtable
    }
    auto t4 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

    auto crtp_us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(t2 - t1).count();
    auto virt_us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(t4 - t3).count();

    std::cout << "CRTP:    " << crtp_total << " in " << crtp_us << " us\n";
    std::cout << "Virtual: " << virt_total << " in " << virt_us << " us\n";

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement policy-based design in C++ using templates to create a configurable smart pointer that supports different threading, storage, and checking policies.
output:
<think>
Policy-based design (from Andrei Alexandrescu's "Modern C++ Design") decomposes a class's behavior into orthogonal policies, each implemented as a template parameter. This gives combinatorial flexibility without inheritance hierarchies.

For a smart pointer, the orthogonal policies are:
1. Ownership: how the pointer manages lifetime (unique, ref-counted, deep-copy)
2. Threading: how ref-counting is synchronized (single-thread, mutex, atomic)
3. Checking: how null dereference is handled (no-check, assert, throw)
4. Storage: how the raw pointer is stored (raw, with deleter, array)

Each policy is a small class with a known interface. The smart pointer inherits from all policies (EBCO — Empty Base Class Optimization means no size overhead for stateless policies).

Design:
- SmartPtr<T, OwnershipPolicy, CheckingPolicy, ThreadingPolicy>
- Each policy is a class template parameterized on T
- The host class (SmartPtr) calls policy methods at appropriate points
- Users can mix and match: SmartPtr<Widget, RefCounted, ThrowOnNull, AtomicThreading>

This is fundamentally different from strategy pattern: policies are resolved at compile time (zero overhead), strategies at runtime (virtual dispatch).

The power is in composition: 3 ownership x 3 checking x 2 threading = 18 distinct smart pointers from 8 small policy classes.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <atomic>
#include <mutex>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <cassert>

// =============================================
// Threading Policies
// =============================================

template <typename T>
class SingleThreaded {
protected:
    using AtomicInt = int;  // Plain int, no sync
    static void increment(AtomicInt& count) { ++count; }
    static int decrement(AtomicInt& count) { return --count; }
};

template <typename T>
class AtomicThreading {
protected:
    using AtomicInt = std::atomic<int>;
    static void increment(AtomicInt& count) {
        count.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed);
    }
    static int decrement(AtomicInt& count) {
        return count.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_acq_rel) - 1;
    }
};

// =============================================
// Checking Policies
// =============================================

template <typename T>
class NoChecking {
protected:
    static void check(T* ptr) { /* no-op */ }
};

template <typename T>
class AssertCheck {
protected:
    static void check(T* ptr) {
        assert(ptr != nullptr && "Null pointer dereference");
    }
};

template <typename T>
class ThrowOnNull {
protected:
    static void check(T* ptr) {
        if (!ptr) throw std::runtime_error("Null pointer dereference");
    }
};

// =============================================
// Ownership Policies
// =============================================

template <typename T, typename Threading>
class RefCounted : public Threading {
protected:
    struct CountBlock {
        typename Threading::AtomicInt count;
        CountBlock() : count(1) {}
    };

    CountBlock* block_ = nullptr;

    void init(T*) {
        block_ = new CountBlock();
    }

    void acquire() {
        if (block_) Threading::increment(block_->count);
    }

    bool release() {
        if (block_ && Threading::decrement(block_->count) == 0) {
            delete block_;
            block_ = nullptr;
            return true; // caller should delete pointee
        }
        return false;
    }
};

// =============================================
// Policy-based SmartPtr
// =============================================

template <
    typename T,
    template <typename, typename> class OwnershipPolicy = RefCounted,
    template <typename> class CheckingPolicy = ThrowOnNull,
    template <typename> class ThreadingPolicy = AtomicThreading
>
class SmartPtr
    : public OwnershipPolicy<T, ThreadingPolicy<T>>
    , public CheckingPolicy<T>
{
    using Owner = OwnershipPolicy<T, ThreadingPolicy<T>>;
    using Check = CheckingPolicy<T>;

    T* ptr_ = nullptr;

public:
    explicit SmartPtr(T* ptr = nullptr) : ptr_(ptr) {
        if (ptr_) Owner::init(ptr_);
    }

    SmartPtr(const SmartPtr& other) : ptr_(other.ptr_) {
        Owner::block_ = other.block_;
        Owner::acquire();
    }

    SmartPtr& operator=(const SmartPtr& other) {
        if (this != &other) {
            if (Owner::release()) delete ptr_;
            ptr_ = other.ptr_;
            Owner::block_ = other.block_;
            Owner::acquire();
        }
        return *this;
    }

    ~SmartPtr() {
        if (Owner::release()) delete ptr_;
    }

    T& operator*() const {
        Check::check(ptr_);
        return *ptr_;
    }

    T* operator->() const {
        Check::check(ptr_);
        return ptr_;
    }

    explicit operator bool() const { return ptr_ != nullptr; }
};

// --- Usage ---

struct Widget {
    int value;
    Widget(int v) : value(v) { std::cout << "Widget(" << v << ")\n"; }
    ~Widget() { std::cout << "~Widget(" << value << ")\n"; }
};

int main() {
    // Thread-safe, throws on null
    using SafePtr = SmartPtr<Widget, RefCounted, ThrowOnNull, AtomicThreading>;

    // Single-threaded, assert on null
    using FastPtr = SmartPtr<Widget, RefCounted, AssertCheck, SingleThreaded>;

    {
        SafePtr p1(new Widget(42));
        SafePtr p2 = p1;  // Ref count = 2
        std::cout << "Value: " << p2->value << "\n";
    } // Widget destroyed once when last ref drops

    try {
        SafePtr null_ptr;
        *null_ptr; // Throws
    } catch (const std::runtime_error& e) {
        std::cout << "Caught: " << e.what() << "\n";
    }

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement expression templates in C++ to enable lazy evaluation of vector arithmetic, avoiding temporary allocations for chained operations like a + b * c.
output:
<think>
Expression templates are a C++ metaprogramming technique that builds an expression tree at compile time instead of eagerly evaluating operations. When you write `a + b * c`, instead of creating temporaries for each operation, the compiler builds a type like `VecAdd<Vec, VecMul<Vec, Vec>>` that evaluates element-by-element when assigned.

Why this matters:
- Without ET: `a + b * c` creates two temporary vectors (one for b*c, one for the addition)
- With ET: the entire expression evaluates in a single pass with no temporaries
- For large vectors, this eliminates O(n) allocations and extra memory passes

Design:
1. Define expression types (VecAdd, VecMul, VecScale) that store references to operands
2. Each expression type has `operator[](size_t i)` that computes the result lazily
3. Operator overloads return expression objects, not evaluated vectors
4. Assignment operator or constructor triggers evaluation in one pass
5. Use CRTP or concepts to constrain expression types

Key insight: the expression template types are typically tiny (just references/pointers) and are completely optimized away by the compiler. The generated assembly is identical to a hand-written loop.

This is how Eigen, Blaze, and xtensor work internally. The technique was invented by Todd Veldhuizen in 1995.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cassert>
#include <chrono>

// Base expression type using CRTP
template <typename E>
class VecExpr {
public:
    double operator[](size_t i) const {
        return static_cast<const E&>(*this)[i];
    }

    size_t size() const {
        return static_cast<const E&>(*this).size();
    }
};

// Concrete vector (owns data)
class Vec : public VecExpr<Vec> {
    std::vector<double> data_;
public:
    explicit Vec(size_t n, double val = 0.0) : data_(n, val) {}

    // Evaluate any expression into this vector
    template <typename E>
    Vec(const VecExpr<E>& expr) : data_(expr.size()) {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < data_.size(); ++i) {
            data_[i] = expr[i];  // Single-pass evaluation
        }
    }

    template <typename E>
    Vec& operator=(const VecExpr<E>& expr) {
        assert(data_.size() == expr.size());
        for (size_t i = 0; i < data_.size(); ++i) {
            data_[i] = expr[i];
        }
        return *this;
    }

    double  operator[](size_t i) const { return data_[i]; }
    double& operator[](size_t i)       { return data_[i]; }
    size_t  size() const               { return data_.size(); }
};

// Expression: element-wise addition
template <typename L, typename R>
class VecAdd : public VecExpr<VecAdd<L, R>> {
    const L& lhs_;
    const R& rhs_;
public:
    VecAdd(const L& l, const R& r) : lhs_(l), rhs_(r) {
        assert(l.size() == r.size());
    }
    double operator[](size_t i) const { return lhs_[i] + rhs_[i]; }
    size_t size() const { return lhs_.size(); }
};

// Expression: element-wise multiplication
template <typename L, typename R>
class VecMul : public VecExpr<VecMul<L, R>> {
    const L& lhs_;
    const R& rhs_;
public:
    VecMul(const L& l, const R& r) : lhs_(l), rhs_(r) {}
    double operator[](size_t i) const { return lhs_[i] * rhs_[i]; }
    size_t size() const { return lhs_.size(); }
};

// Expression: scalar multiply
template <typename E>
class VecScale : public VecExpr<VecScale<E>> {
    double scalar_;
    const E& expr_;
public:
    VecScale(double s, const E& e) : scalar_(s), expr_(e) {}
    double operator[](size_t i) const { return scalar_ * expr_[i]; }
    size_t size() const { return expr_.size(); }
};

// Operator overloads return expression templates, not evaluated vectors
template <typename L, typename R>
VecAdd<L, R> operator+(const VecExpr<L>& l, const VecExpr<R>& r) {
    return VecAdd<L, R>(static_cast<const L&>(l), static_cast<const R&>(r));
}

template <typename L, typename R>
VecMul<L, R> operator*(const VecExpr<L>& l, const VecExpr<R>& r) {
    return VecMul<L, R>(static_cast<const L&>(l), static_cast<const R&>(r));
}

template <typename E>
VecScale<E> operator*(double s, const VecExpr<E>& e) {
    return VecScale<E>(s, static_cast<const E&>(e));
}

int main() {
    const size_t N = 1'000'000;
    Vec a(N, 1.0), b(N, 2.0), c(N, 3.0);

    // Expression template: NO temporaries created
    // Type is Vec = VecAdd<Vec, VecAdd<VecMul<Vec, Vec>, VecScale<Vec>>>
    // Evaluates in a single pass: result[i] = a[i] + b[i]*c[i] + 0.5*a[i]
    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    Vec result = a + b * c + 0.5 * a;
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

    auto us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(t2 - t1).count();
    std::cout << "result[0] = " << result[0] << " (expected 7.5)\n";
    std::cout << "result[999999] = " << result[999999] << "\n";
    std::cout << "Time: " << us << " us (single pass, zero temporaries)\n";

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement tag dispatch in C++ to select different algorithm implementations based on iterator category, providing optimized versions for random access vs forward iterators.
output:
<think>
Tag dispatch is a classic C++ technique that uses overload resolution with tag types to select the right implementation at compile time. The STL uses this internally — std::advance has different implementations for random-access iterators (O(1) pointer arithmetic) vs forward iterators (O(n) increment loop).

How it works:
1. Iterator categories form a hierarchy of empty tag structs
2. std::iterator_traits<Iter>::iterator_category gives the tag type
3. We write overloaded helper functions that take the tag as a parameter
4. The compiler selects the right overload based on the tag type
5. The tag parameter is unused at runtime — it only drives overload resolution

This is different from if-constexpr (C++17) which also works but tag dispatch is more idiomatic in pre-C++17 code and sometimes clearer when you have many overloads.

Design: implement a `distance` function and a `rotate` function with different strategies:
- RandomAccessIterator: use pointer arithmetic, O(1)
- BidirectionalIterator: can decrement, enables certain optimizations
- ForwardIterator: can only increment, most conservative

I'll also show the C++20 concepts approach as the modern alternative.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <list>
#include <forward_list>
#include <iterator>
#include <algorithm>
#include <chrono>

// =============================================
// Tag dispatch: select algorithm by iterator type
// =============================================

namespace detail {

// Advance: O(1) for random access
template <typename Iter>
void advance_impl(Iter& it, typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::difference_type n,
                  std::random_access_iterator_tag) {
    it += n;  // Single pointer arithmetic operation
}

// Advance: O(n) for bidirectional (can go backwards)
template <typename Iter>
void advance_impl(Iter& it, typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::difference_type n,
                  std::bidirectional_iterator_tag) {
    if (n > 0) {
        while (n--) ++it;
    } else {
        while (n++) --it;
    }
}

// Advance: O(n) for forward (only forward)
template <typename Iter>
void advance_impl(Iter& it, typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::difference_type n,
                  std::forward_iterator_tag) {
    while (n-- > 0) ++it;
}

// Nth element: O(1) for random access, O(n) otherwise
template <typename Iter>
typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::reference
nth_impl(Iter first, size_t n, std::random_access_iterator_tag) {
    return first[n];  // Direct indexing
}

template <typename Iter>
typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::reference
nth_impl(Iter first, size_t n, std::forward_iterator_tag) {
    while (n-- > 0) ++first;
    return *first;
}

// Sort: different strategies
template <typename Iter>
void sort_impl(Iter first, Iter last, std::random_access_iterator_tag) {
    std::sort(first, last);  // Introsort: O(n log n), requires RA
    std::cout << "[Using std::sort - random access]\n";
}

template <typename Iter>
void sort_impl(Iter first, Iter last, std::forward_iterator_tag) {
    // Can't use std::sort, collect into vector, sort, copy back
    using T = typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::value_type;
    std::vector<T> tmp(first, last);
    std::sort(tmp.begin(), tmp.end());
    std::copy(tmp.begin(), tmp.end(), first);
    std::cout << "[Using copy-sort-copy - forward only]\n";
}

} // namespace detail

// Public API: tag dispatch happens automatically
template <typename Iter>
void my_advance(Iter& it, typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::difference_type n) {
    detail::advance_impl(it, n,
        typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::iterator_category{});
}

template <typename Iter>
auto my_nth(Iter first, size_t n) -> typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::reference {
    return detail::nth_impl(first, n,
        typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::iterator_category{});
}

template <typename Iter>
void my_sort(Iter first, Iter last) {
    detail::sort_impl(first, last,
        typename std::iterator_traits<Iter>::iterator_category{});
}

int main() {
    // Random access (vector) — gets O(1) advance, direct indexing
    std::vector<int> vec = {5, 3, 1, 4, 2};
    auto vit = vec.begin();
    my_advance(vit, 3);
    std::cout << "vec[3] via advance: " << *vit << "\n";
    std::cout << "vec[2] via nth: " << my_nth(vec.begin(), 2) << "\n";
    my_sort(vec.begin(), vec.end());

    // Forward only (forward_list) — gets O(n) fallbacks
    std::forward_list<int> fwd = {5, 3, 1, 4, 2};
    auto fit = fwd.begin();
    my_advance(fit, 2);
    std::cout << "fwd[2] via advance: " << *fit << "\n";
    my_sort(fwd.begin(), fwd.end());

    // Print sorted results
    std::cout << "Sorted vec: ";
    for (int x : vec) std::cout << x << " ";
    std::cout << "\nSorted fwd: ";
    for (int x : fwd) std::cout << x << " ";
    std::cout << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```
